title: Assessment of quality of commercial hand sanitizers using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and gas chromatography
author: Alam, Rahat, et al.
description: Since the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic, the use and manufacture of alcohol-based hand sanitizers increased exponentially. Efficacy of hand sanitizers mainly depends on active ingredients like ethanol and isopropanol (IPA). Even though methanol is extremely hazardous to people, it is still illegally used in hand sanitizers in Bangladesh. Developing a quick and simple analytical method for detecting and quantifying ethanol/IPA/methanol is crucial. Here, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to identify and quantify alcohol content in commercially available hand sanitizers in a quick and easy way. Comparing the FTIR and GC data, provided quite similar results. Unlike previous studies by FTIR, C‚ÄìH, CH3‚ÄìC‚ÄìCH3 stretching, and C‚ÄìH bending vibrational modes were employed to construct analytical calibration curves to detect and quantify alcohol in hand sanitizers. According to FTIR and GC findings, ethanol and IPA content were found to be 43‚Äì82% and 40‚Äì69%, and 56‚Äì64% and 61‚Äì66%, respectively, whereas ethanol was labeled at 66‚Äì80% and IPA at 65‚Äì70%. FTIR and GC revealed methanol content ranging from 37 to 98 and 19 to 81%, respectively. Also, the FTIR was significantly faster than the GC. Therefore, FTIR can be used to commercially analyze the quality of hand sanitizers.‚Ä¢FTIR was used to identify and quantify alcohol content in commercially available hand sanitizers in a quick and easy way.‚Ä¢Comparing the FTIR and GC data, provided quite similar results.‚Ä¢Out of ten samples, five contained ethanol, three IPA, and two methanol.

steps_raw.title: Methods
steps_raw.instruction: Calcium fluoride crystal plates were used as sample holder for liquid samples in infrared (IR) spectroscopy. A sample holder made of calcium fluoride crystals is shown in Figure S2. Calibration standards were prepared by diluting each absolute alcohol with water in the following proportions: 100/0, 80/20, 70/30, 60/40, and 50/50. Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (FTIR) (IRPrestige-21, SHIMADZU, Japan) was used to record the IR spectra of the calibration standards and the commercial hand sanitizers within a window range of 4000 to 400 cm‚àí1 prior to analysis of the real sanitizer samples. About 20‚Äì30 ŒºL of the standard was directly introduced into a capillary of the sample holder and then placed on the sample window of the FTIR instrument and scanned to record the IR spectrum. Each standard was scanned at least three times (n¬†=¬†3).

steps_raw.title: Analysis of commercial hand sanitizers
steps_raw.instruction: In a similar way, about 20‚Äì30 ŒºL of the relevant sanitizer sample was directly loaded into a capillary of the sample holder and placed on the sample window of the FTIR instrument and then scanned. The raw data were transferred to a desktop and edited using Excel in order to re-generate the relevant spectrum. Similarly, the IR spectrum for each sample was scanned at least three times (n¬†=¬†3). The average peak heights were obtained from the spectra and used for determining the alcohol content.

steps_raw.title: GC-FID analysis of the samples
steps_raw.instruction: The content of ethanol, IPA and methanol were determined using a gas chromatography-flame ionization detector (GC-FID) system. Separation was achieved by GC column SCION-5 (5%-phenyl-methylpolysiloxane, internal diameter 30¬†m¬†√ó¬†0.25¬†mm and film thickness 0.25¬†¬µm) at a flow rate of 1.5¬†mL/min.

steps_raw.title: GC-FID analysis of the samples
steps_raw.instruction: To construct calibration curves, working standard solutions of ethanol, IPA, and methanol at various concentrations were prepared in toluene. Each of the hand sanitizer samples was diluted with toluene 25 times. Exactly, 0.01¬µL sample/standard was injected by auto injector to the column. All of these analyses were carried out in triplicate (n¬†=¬†3) to ensure that the experiment was of high quality. Table S1 provides more information. Fig.¬†1 shows a scheme of experimental protocol for sample preparation and analysis using (a) FTIR and (b) GC-FID techniques, respectively.Fig. 1A scheme of experimental protocol for sample preparation and analysis using (a) FTIR and (b) GC-FID techniques, respectively.Fig. 1

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Alcohol content, standard deviation (SD), relative standard deviation (RSD) and linear range correlation coefficient (R2) were measured to validate the IR spectroscopy as an analytical method [24]. For each standard curve, at least three peak heights (n¬†=¬†3) were used, and the generated curve was taken into account if the square correlation coefficients (R2) were greater than 0.905. The calibration curves were constructed using the average peak heights of the relevant alcohol's vibrational modes. Table¬†1 lists the pertinent modes of vibration, their wave numbers and the linear range correlation coefficient (R2) for each alcohol.Table 1Construction of analytical calibration curves. .Table 1AlcoholModes of vibrationWave number (cm‚àí1)R2EthanolC‚àíH stretching29760.9527C‚àíH bending8810.9801C‚àíO stretching10500.9051IPAC‚àíH stretching29700.9129CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3 bending9520.9222MethanolC‚àíH stretching29430.9039C‚àíO stretching10320.9963

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: The relevant analytical calibration curves are shown in Figure S3 (C‚àíO stretching, 1050 cm‚àí1, for ethanol), Figures S4(a) and (b) (C‚àíH stretching, 2976 cm‚àí1, and C‚àíH bending, 881 cm‚àí1 for ethanol), Figures S5(a) and (b) (C‚àíH stretching, 2970 cm‚àí1, and CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3, 952 cm‚àí1, for IPA) and Figures S6(a) and (b) (C‚àíH stretching, 2943 cm‚àí1, and C‚àíO stretching, 1032 cm‚àí1, for methanol).

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Figures S7(a), (b) and (c) show the IR spectra of pure ethanol, IPA and methanol, respectively. As seen from Figures S7(a), (b) and (c), red and blue circles indicate C‚àíH and C‚àíO stretching and the purple circle shows C-H bending while green and orange circles represent ‚àíC(CH3)2 bending and C‚àíC‚àíO symmetric stretching, respectively [18]. In this study, these functional groups were considered in order to detect and quantify the different alcohols in alcohol-based commercial hand sanitizers. The C‚àíO stretching vibrational mode, which appears between 1075 and 1350 cm‚àí1, has been identified as a promising group for quantifying ethanol, isopropanol, and even methanol in commercial hand sanitizers [18,25]. Hydrogen bonding in alcoholic compounds plays vital role for the existence of alcohols as liquid. Water molecules in alcohols also forms hydrogen bonding with the alcoholic molecules. So, the presence of water alters the C‚àíO group's intensity as well as its peak position. This is why the C‚àíO stretching has been¬†used as a key parameter in the construction of analytical calibration curves [25].

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Water, on the other hand, has no direct interaction with the non-polar C‚àíH moiety in alcohol, but it does affect the intensity of the C‚àíH groups. In order to identify and quantify ethanol, isopropanol, and methanol in hand sanitizers, the C‚àíO and C‚àíH stretching and C‚àíH bending vibrational modes, as well as the CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3 bending mode for isopropanol, were taken into account in the construction of analytical calibration curves.

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Figs.¬†2(a) and (b) show the IR spectra of ethanol standards and commercial brands A, B, C, D and E with ethanol standards of 50, 60, and 70%, from 980 to 1140 cm‚àí1, respectively. The C‚àíO group's absorbances centered at about 1050 cm‚àí1 for various ethanol/water ratios were used to construct an analytical curve, as illustrated in Figure S7. The calibration curve had a moderately good correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.953 (Figure S3). As indicated in Fig.¬†2(b), the ethanol content of brands A, B, C, D, and E are 59, 63, 62, 35, and 45%, respectively, while the labeled values were 66, 70, 80, 70, 66%. The values are also tabulated in Table¬†2. The results indicate that the ethanol content of all the commercial hand sanitizers was lower than the labeled values. The results were compared to those obtained by GC and also found to be lower than the reported values (Table¬†2).Fig. 2IR spectra of (a) ethanol standards and (b) commercial brands A, B, C, D, E and F with ethanol standards 50, 60 and 70%.Fig. 2Table 2Concentration of ethanol, isopropanol and methanol in various commercial hand sanitizers.Table 2SampleTypeConcentration (%)LabeledMeasuredFTIRGCC‚àíO stretching (1000‚Äì1140 cm‚àí1)C‚àíH stretching (2976 cm‚àí1)C‚àíH bending(881 cm‚àí1)AEthanol665933‚Äì3442‚Äì4340BEthanol706362‚Äì6360‚Äì6169CEthanol8062787065DEthanol70357975‚Äì7645EEthanol664557‚Äì5856442970 cm‚àí1CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3 stretching (952 cm‚àí1)FIPA65‚Äì5658‚Äì5961GIPA70‚Äì64‚Äì656461HIPA70‚Äì57‚Äì5860‚Äì61661032 cm‚àí12943 cm‚àí1IMethanol709898‚Äì81JMethanolMentioned as alcohol37‚Äì3839‚Äì19

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: The IR spectra of ethanol with varying water content (a) 0, (b) 20, (c) 30, (d) 40, and (e) 50 percent are shown in Figs.¬†3(a)-(e). The red and blue marks indicate how ethanol/water ratios affect the intensities of C‚àíH stretching (2975 cm‚àí1) and C‚àíH bending (881¬†cm-1) vibrational modes, respectively. The height of both peaks (C‚àíH stretching and C‚àíH bending) decreases with increasing water content, as illustrated in Figs.¬†3(a)-(e). Accordingly, two analytical calibration curves based on variation in heights of the C‚àíH stretching and C‚àíH bending vibration modes were constructed, as illustrated in Figures S4(a) and (b). For the two curves, the correlation coefficients (R2) were 0.905 for C-H stretching and 0.980 for C‚àíH bending vibration mode. According to the findings, using the C‚àíH bending vibration mode to quantify ethanol in hand sanitizers could be more precise.Fig. 3IR spectra of ethanol with different content of water (a) 0, (b) 20, (c) 30, (d) 40 and (e) 50%. The red and blue marks are for variation of heights of C‚àíH stretching (‚àº2975 cm‚àí1) and C‚àíH bending (881 cm‚àí1) vibrational modes with ethanol/water ratios, respectively. RSD are in the parentheses.Fig. 3

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: With the exception of sample D, the content of ethanol in commercial hand sanitizers was lower than the reported values, as revealed by the analytical curves in Figures S4(a) and (b). Table¬†2 and Figs.¬†4(a)-(e) show the results. The results were compared to those of GC. For samples A, B, and C, the ethanol content was comparable using these two methods, FTIR and GC, where¬†the C‚àíH bending vibration-based analytical curve exhibiting more precise results (Table¬†2). However, the ethanol content of samples D and E was much higher by both calibration curves (C‚àíH stretching and C‚àíH bending) than those examined by GC, but they were quite close to the labeled values. The ethanol content measured by the C‚àíO stretching calibration curve was similar to that measured by GC, however it was significantly lower than the reported values (Table¬†2).Fig. 4IR spectra of commercial hand sanitizers (a) A, (b) B, (c) C, (d) D and (e) F. The red and blue marks are for variation of heights of C‚àíH stretching (‚àº2975 cm‚àí1) and C‚àíH bending (881 cm‚àí1) vibrational modes with ethanol/water ratios, respectively. RSD are in the parentheses.Fig. 4

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: According to WHO formulations, hand sanitizers are supposed to contain ethanol (75‚Äì80%) or IPA (70‚Äì75%), as well as glycerol (1.45%), hydrogen peroxide (0.125%), and water. Because both water and glycerin molecules make hydrogen bonds with ethanol molecules through the C‚àíO‚àíH group of ethanol and H‚àíO‚àíH and/or C‚àíO‚àíH of glycerin, the C‚àíO stretching mode of alcohol can be dramatically changed by adding polar compounds like water, glycerin, and so on. This is why the C‚àíO stretching mode has been used to determine the amount of alcohol in hand sanitizers [18,25].

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Polar compounds, such as water or glycerin, on the other hand, do not interact with the C‚àíH group of alcoholic compounds and instead reduce the peak height when they are added. As a result, employing the analytical calibration curves derived by C‚àíH bending and/or C‚àíH stretching to quantify alcoholic substances such as ethanol, isopropanol, and/or methanol in hand sanitizers is scientifically plausible.

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: The IR spectra of isopropanol (IPA) with 0, 30, 40, 50, and 60 percent water are shown in Figs.¬†5(a)-(e), respectively. The spectral pattern of the IPA has substantially changed when water is added, as shown in Figs.¬†5(a)-(e). When water was added, the intensities of both the C‚àíH and C‚àíO stretching vibrational modes decreased dramatically, in contrast to ethanol. The bending vibration of the CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3 group caused an additional peak for IPA at 952 cm‚àí1. With the addition of water, the intensity of this peak was also reduced. As shown in Figures S5(a) and (b), C‚àíH (2970 cm‚àí1) and CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3 (952 cm‚àí1) bending were used to construct analytical calibration curves for detecting and quantifying IPA in commercial hand sanitizers (Table¬†1). In the calibration curves, the correlation coefficients (R2) for C‚àíH and CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3 bending were found to be 0.913 and 0.922, respectively. This is due to the fact that the presence of polar water molecules has a considerable impact on the C‚àíH and CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3 bending vibration modes of IPA, resulting in a regular fluctuation in peak height with the addition of water.Fig. 5IR spectra of isopropanol (IPA) with different content of water (a) 0, (b) 30, (c) 40, (d) 50 and (e) 40%. The red and blue marks are for variation of heights of C‚àíH stretching (‚àº2975 cm‚àí1) and CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3 bending (952 cm‚àí1) vibrational modes with IPA/water ratios, respectively. RSD are in the parentheses.Fig. 5

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: Like ethanol, an analytical calibration curve based on C‚àíO stretching was also constructed for IPA and correlation coefficient (R2) was found to be less than 0.700 (data not shown). Density functional theory (DFT) calculation supported the deformation of IPA molecules in presence of water [26]. They reported that the aqua-IPA changes their structure from a planar ring structure to a three-dimensional cage structure when the number of water molecules around IPA become 5 (IPA-(H2O)5). Unlike ethanol, IPA molecules are less polar because of presence of two methyl group. So, intensities of the both C‚àíH and CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3 bending are sensible in presence of water, accordingly two analytical curves were constructed for IPA.

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: The IR spectra of commercial hand sanitizers of F, G, and H are shown in Figure S8(a), (b), and (c), respectively. IPA content in hand sanitizers was estimated using the two analytical calibration curves (Figure S5(a) and (b)) and listed in Table¬†2. The results show that IPA content based on the CH3‚àíC‚àíCH3 peak is extremely similar to GC values. The C‚àíH stretching-based calibration curve showed similar results to the GC values (Table¬†2). None of the IPA values in commercial hand sanitizers meet the FITR and/or GC-estimated values.

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: As expected, methanol is found in two out of ten hand sanitizers in Bangladesh. Alcohol dehydrogenase converts methanol into formaldehyde, which is then transformed to formic acid by aldehyde dehydrogenase. If ingested at a high enough quantity, it can result in blindness and death. The IR spectra of methanol with 0, 20, 30, and 40% water are shown in Figure S9(a)-(d), respectively. The red and blue marks indicate how methanol/water ratios affect the heights of C‚àíH stretching (2975 cm‚àí1) and C‚àíO stretching (1032 cm‚àí1) vibrational modes, respectively. Unlike IPA, methanol has a characteristic C‚àíO stretching vibration mode that¬†appears at 1032 cm‚àí1 (Figure S9(a)) and subsequently drops in peak height when the amount of water increases (Figures S9(b)-(d)). The height of the C‚àíH stretching (2943 cm‚àí1) mode similarly lowers as the water content increases. In order to identify and quantify methanol in hand sanitizers, both the C‚àíH and C‚àíO stretching modes were used in the construction of two analytical calibration curves. Figures S6(a) and (b) illustrate the calibration curves based on C‚àíH and C‚àíO stretching vibrations, respectively. The correlation coefficients (R2) were found to be 0.904 and 0.996 for C‚àíH and C‚àíO stretching vibrations, respectively.

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: The IR spectra of commercial hand sanitizers I and J are shown in Figure S10(a) and (b), respectively. The red and blue marks represent the heights of the C‚àíH stretching (2943 cm‚àí1) and C‚àíO stretching (1032 cm‚àí1) vibrational modes, respectively, as a function of methanol/water ratios. Methanol content in I and J samples was 98 and 37‚Äì38% using the calibration curve-based on C‚àíO stretching, respectively, while it was 98 and 39% using the analytical curve-based on C‚àíH stretching, as shown in Figures S6(a) and (b) and Table¬†2. However, methanol content in samples I and J was found to be 81 and 19%, respectively, using GC (Table¬†2). According to manufacturers, sample I was written as 70% alcohol instead of methanol, whereas sample J was written as just alcoholic. The IR-based finding is less analytically valid if the methanol content in sample I determined by GC is correct. Adulteration with hydrocarbon-based compounds is offered as another analytical way to evaluate the quality of sample I, based on the high content detected by the IR-based method.

steps_raw.title: Analytical method validation
steps_raw.instruction: In order to evaluate the selectivity of the assigned wavenumbers for the identification of ethanol, IPA¬†and methanol in the commercial¬†hand sanitizers, the effect of additional alcohols and/or volatile organic compounds, such as acetone, 1-propanol, and ethyl acetate, that may be present in such formulations was assessed (data not shown). Since the concentrations of ethanol, IPA, and methanol in the samples are relatively high (30%‚Äì75%), no influence from other alcohols or volatile compounds like acetone, butanol, ethyl acetate, etc. occurs until their concentration level reaches the same order as the alcohols taken into consideration [27]. None of the samples used in the current study included mixed alcohols.

materials: Ethanol, IPA, methanol and acetone were purchased from Active Fine Chemicals Ltd., Bangladesh. Commercially available hand sanitizers were purchased from local drug stores in Dhaka, Bangladesh in order to detect and quantify alcohol content (see Figure S1). Distilled water was used throughout the experiment.
